message.content: check all these projects and research pappers then apply what fits and optimize our project https://www.perplexity.ai/search/you-are-a-research-specialist-o.J49P_RTai8k0VpOpIXew

content.thinking:
The user wants me to check a Perplexity search result and apply relevant findings to optimize their project. Let me first fetch that URL to see what research/papers are referenced, and also understand what project we're working with.

Let me fetch the Perplexity URL and explore the current project directory in parallel.

content.text: Let me fetch that research and explore the current project simultaneously.

"{\"title\":\"Perplexity\",\"url\":\"https://www.perplexity.ai/search/you-are-a-research-specialist-o.J49P_RTai8k0VpOpIXew\",\"content\":\"# You are a research specialist tasked with conducting a comprehensive...\\nSearch ⌥⌘1 Computer ⌥⌘2\\nNew thread\\n⌘K\\nHistory Discover Spaces Finance Health\\nMore\\nRecent\\nRecent and active threads will appear here.\\nAnswer\\nAnswer\\nLinks\\nLinks\\nImages\\nImages\\nShare\\n# You are a research specialist tasked with conducting a comprehensive literature and resource search. Your goal is to identify and compile all available academic papers, open-source repositories, and active projects related to **Inverse Digital Signal Processing (Inverse DSP)** and **Blind Parameter Estimation**. **Search Scope:** Conduct searches across the following sources: - Academic databases: arXiv, IEEE Xplore, Google Scholar, JSTOR, ResearchGate - Code repositories: GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket - Project platforms: Kaggle, Papers with Code, GitHub Discussions - Institutional repositories and university research pages - Conference proceedings (DSP-related conferences: ICASSP, EUSIPCO, GlobalSIP, etc.) **Core Research Topics to Cover:** Include papers and projects addressing: - Inverse DSP methods and algorithms - Blind parameter estimation techniques - Blind system identification - Blind deconvolution - Blind source separation - Parameter recovery from limited or corrupted observations - Machine learning approaches to blind estimation - Applications in audio, communications, seismic, medical imaging, or radar domains **Deliverable Requirements:** Organize findings into a structured compilation that includes: - **Academic Papers**: Title, authors, publication year, venue, DOI/link, brief description of contribution - **Open-Source Repositories**: Repository name, platform, primary language, description, last update date, number of stars/forks where applicable - **Active Projects**: Project name, institution/organization, status, description, relevant links For each item, verify the source is legitimate and the content is accurately described. Cite the specific databases or platforms where you found each resource. **Critical Standards:** - Do not present unverified claims as fact; cite sources explicitly for all findings - Distinguish between papers specifically about Inverse DSP/Blind Parameter Estimation versus tangentially related work - Prioritize recent, high-impact publications alongside foundational works - Flag any papers or projects that are particularly influential or novel in their approach - Note any gaps or underexplored areas you identify in the current landscape Return the compilation in a clear, easy-to-reference format that enables the user to quickly assess the landscape and access resources directly.\\nShow more\\nCompleted 3 steps\\nNow I have enough material to compile a comprehensive and structured report. Let me organize and present it.\\n* * *\\n# Inverse DSP & Blind Parameter Estimation: Research Landscape\\nThis compilation covers the full spectrum of Inverse DSP and Blind Parameter Estimation resources — from foundational theory to cutting-edge 2025/2026 publications and active open-source tooling.\\n* * *\\n## 📌 How to Read This Report\\nResources are classified into three tiers: **directly core** (Inverse DSP / Blind Parameter Estimation as the primary subject), **highly relevant** (methods that directly enable these), and **application-specific** (domain deployments in audio, comms, seismic, etc.).\\n* * *\\n## 📄 Academic Papers\\n## Foundational & Theoretical Works\\n| Title | Authors / Venue | Year | DOI / Link | Classification | Notes |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| **Blind Recovery of Sparse Signals from Subsampled Convolution** | arXiv:1511.06149 | 2015 | arxiv.org/abs/1511.06149 | ⭐ Core | Provides provable guarantees for blind deconvolution using sparsity + spectral flatness priors at near-optimal sample complexity arxiv |\\n| **Variational Bayesian Blind Image Deconvolution: A Review** | Ruiz et al., _Signal Processing_ | 2015 | PDF | ⭐ Core | Comprehensive review of VB-based BID methods; covers SG/SMG priors and sampling approaches; excellent entry point ccia.ugr |\\n| **Sparse Multichannel Blind Deconvolution of Seismic Data via Basis Pursuit** | arXiv:1611.03754 | 2016 | arxiv.org/abs/1611.03754 | ⭐ Core (Seismic) | Iterative wavelet estimation + sparse reflectivity refinement using spectral projected-gradient; multichannel scenario arxiv |\\n## Machine Learning & Neural Approaches\\n| Title | Authors / Venue | Year | DOI / Link | Classification | Notes |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| **DDSP: Differentiable Digital Signal Processing** | Engel et al., _ICLR 2020_ | 2020 | magenta.github.io/ddsp | ⭐ Foundational | Introduced differentiable DSP primitives (synthesizers, filters, effects) enabling gradient-based inverse DSP; highly cited github |\\n| **Style Transfer of Audio Effects with Differentiable Signal Processing** | arXiv:2207.08759 | 2022 | arxiv.org/abs/2207.08759 | ⭐ Core | DNN predicts DSP control parameters from input + reference style; neural proxy for backward pass; directly addresses inverse AFX estimation arxiv |\\n| **Blind Equalization and Channel Estimation in Coherent Optical Communications** | arXiv:2204.11776 | 2022 | arxiv.org/abs/2204.11776 | ⭐ Core (Comms) | VAE-based blind equalizer generalized to PCS, oversampling, dual-polarization; outperforms CMA for time-varying channels arxiv |\\n| **Unsupervised Blind Source Separation with Variational Auto-Encoders** | Neri et al., _EUSIPCO 2021_ | 2021 | github.com/jundsp/VAE-BSS | Highly Relevant | Unsupervised VAE framework for BSS without labelled data github |\\n| **Low-Complexity Blind Parameter Estimation in Wireless Systems with Noisy Sparse Signals** | arXiv:2302.14089 | 2023 | arxiv.org/abs/2302.14089 | ⭐ Core | Novel blind estimators for noise power, SNR, MSE leveraging large-dimensional sparse data; demonstrated in mm-wave and cell-free systems arxiv |\\n| **Blind Channel Equalization Using VQ-VAE** | arXiv:2302.11687 | 2023 | arxiv.org/abs/2302.11687 | ⭐ Core (Comms) | VQ-VAE framework for blind channel equalization extended to nonlinear systems with high-order modulation; novel codebook-based loss arxiv |\\n| **Blind Acoustic Room Parameter Estimation Using Phase Features** | arXiv:2303.07449 | 2023 | arxiv.org/abs/2303.07449 | ⭐ Core (Audio) | Extends reverberation fingerprint estimation (RT60, volume) using phase-related features; outperforms magnitude-only baselines arxiv |\\n| **Blind Estimation of Audio Effects Using an Auto-Encoder Approach** | Peladeau et al., _ICASSP 2024_ | 2023/2024 | arxiv.org/abs/2310.11781 | ⭐ Core (Audio) | BE-AFX auto-encoder using audio quality metric; eliminates need for known AFX implementation; uses DDSP or neural proxies for mastering chain arxiv |\\n| **A Review of Differentiable DSP for Music and Speech** | _Frontiers in Signal Processing_ | 2024 | frontiersin.org/…/10.3389/frsip.2023.1284100 | Highly Relevant | Comprehensive 2024 survey cataloguing all DDSP applications; essential reference for the inverse DSP landscape in audio frontiersin |\\n| **BERP: A Blind Estimator of Room Parameters for Single-Channel** | arXiv:2405.04476 | 2024 | arxiv.org/abs/2405.04476 | ⭐ Core (Audio) | Multitask learning framework for blindly estimating full set of room acoustic + geometric parameters from single-channel speech arxiv+1 |\\n| **Unsupervised Blind Joint Dereverberation and Room Acoustics Estimation (BUDDy)** | arXiv:2408.07472 | 2024 | arxiv.org/abs/2408.07472 | ⭐ Core (Audio) | Unsupervised single-channel blind dereverberation + RIR estimation via score-based diffusion; parametric filter with per-subband exponential decay arxiv |\\n| **Blind Channel Estimation via Baum-Welch over ISI Channels** | arXiv:2412.07907 | 2024 | arxiv.org/pdf/2412.07907.pdf | ⭐ Core (Comms) | Modified BW estimator with reconstructed trellis reducing state count by half; joint turbo-BW-equalization system arxiv |\\n| **Deep Learning Approaches for Channel Length Estimation in Blind SIMO Systems** | _Signal Processing_ (Elsevier) | 2025 | doi.org/10.1016/j.sigpro… | ⭐ Core (Comms) | DL method using symmetric properties of SIMO channel for blind length estimation; enables downstream blind equalization sciencedirect |\\n| **CPA-Net: Blind Joint Signal Parameter Estimation via Channel-Wise Attention** | _IEEE Access_ | 2025 | ieeexplore.ieee.org/…/11264543 | ⭐ Core (Comms) | Dual-branch deep learning framework for joint blind estimation of symbol rate and CFO ieeexplore.ieee |\\n| **Audio Processor Parameters: Blind Estimation (DAFx 2025)** | _DAFx 2025_ | 2025 | dafx25.dii.univpm.it/…/paper_19.pdf | ⭐ Core (Audio) | Neural network-optimized blind estimation of audio processor parameters; presented at DAFx 2025 dafx25.dii.univpm |\\n| **Matching Reverberant Speech Through Learned Acoustic Embeddings and FDNs** | arXiv:2510.23158 | 2025 | arxiv.org/abs/2510.23158 | ⭐ Core (Audio) | Blind estimation of FDN reverberation parameters via learned room-acoustic prior; differentiable FDN with frequency-dependent decay arxiv |\\n| **Plug-and-Play Posterior Sampling for Blind Inverse Problems (Blind-PnPDM)** | arXiv:2505.22923 | 2025 | arxiv.org/abs/2505.22923 | ⭐ Core | Dual diffusion model framework: one for target image prior, one for measurement operator prior; state-of-the-art on blind image deblurring arxiv |\\n| **Estimation of Instrument and Noise Parameters for Inverse Problems** | arXiv:2602.11711 | 2026 | arxiv.org/pdf/2602.11711.pdf | ⭐ Core | Addresses joint estimation of observation system response + error parameters in inverse problems (myopic/semi-blind inversion) arxiv |\\n| **Gibbs Posterior Sampler for Inverse Problems Based on Diffusion Prior (G-DSP)** | arXiv:2602.11059 | 2026 | arxiv.org/html/2602.11059v1 | Highly Relevant | Bayesian inversion using diffusion-model prior; efficient Gibbs sampling; MNIST image deconvolution demo; discusses path to hyperparameter + instrument estimation arxiv |\\n* * *\\n## 🛠️ Open-Source Repositories\\n| Repository | Platform | Language | Description | Stars/Activity | Link |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| **magenta/ddsp** | GitHub | Python (TF) | Google Magenta's DDSP library — differentiable synthesizers, filters, reverb, effects; the foundational toolkit for gradient-based inverse DSP in audio | ⭐ 3k+, actively maintained github | github.com/magenta/ddsp |\\n| **csteinmetz1/dasp-pytorch** | GitHub | Python (PyTorch) | Differentiable Audio Signal Processors — explicitly designed for **blind parameter estimation**, automated DSP, and style transfer; GPU batching support | Actively maintained github | github.com/csteinmetz1/dasp-pytorch |\\n| **peladeaucome/ICASSP-2024-BEAFX-using-DDSP** | GitHub | Python | Official code for ICASSP 2024 paper on blind audio effect estimation using auto-encoder + DDSP; mastering chain AFX estimation | Research release github | github.com/peladeaucome/ICASSP-2024-BEAFX-using-DDSP |\\n| **acids-ircam/ddsp_pytorch** | GitHub | Python (PyTorch) | IRCAM's PyTorch DDSP implementation exportable to TorchScript for real-time environments; supports inverse synthesis and parameter estimation | Active github | github.com/acids-ircam/ddsp_pytorch |\\n| **sai-soum/Diff-MST** | GitHub | Python (PyTorch) | Multitrack Music Style Transfer via differentiable mixing console; predicts EQ/compression/panning parameters from reference audio — a full inverse mixing pipeline | Active github | github.com/sai-soum/Diff-MST |\\n| **fgnt/pb_bss** | GitHub | Python | Collection of EM algorithms for blind source separation in multi-channel speech; integrates with Deep Clustering embeddings | Published (Interspeech 2017) github | github.com/fgnt/pb_bss |\\n| **jundsp/VAE-BSS** | GitHub | Python (PyTorch) | Unsupervised VAE for blind source separation; EUSIPCO 2021; no labelled data required | Research release github | github.com/jundsp/VAE-BSS |\\n| **ssprl/Real-time-Blind-source-separation-using-IVA** | GitHub | Python/MATLAB | Real-time BSS via Independent Vector Analysis with NN-based sound source localization; optimized for hearing improvement applications | Published 2021 github | github.com/ssprl/Real-time-Blind-source-separation-using-IVA |\\n| **DavideNardone/Blind-Source-Separation-using-Dictionary-Learning** | GitHub | Python/MATLAB | BSS via sparse dictionary learning and mixing matrix estimation; block signal representation for computation efficiency | Archived (2017) github | github.com/DavideNardone/Blind-Source-Separation-using-Dictionary-Learning |\\n* * *\\n## 🔬 Active Projects & Frameworks\\n| Project | Institution / Org | Status | Description | Link |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| **DDSP (Differentiable DSP)** | Google Magenta | ✅ Active | Ongoing development of differentiable audio synthesis and processing primitives; widely adopted as the backbone of inverse audio DSP research github | magenta.github.io/ddsp |\\n| **BERP** | Academic (arXiv authors) | ✅ Active (2024–2025) | Universal framework for blind room parameter estimation; SOTA across acoustic and geometric parameters from single-channel recordings arxiv | arxiv.org/abs/2405.04476 |\\n| **BUDDy (Blind Unsupervised Dereverberation)** | Academic | ✅ Active (2024) | Score-based diffusion approach to joint blind dereverberation + RIR estimation; unsupervised and single-channel arxiv | arxiv.org/abs/2408.07472 |\\n| **Blind-PnPDM** | Academic | ✅ Active (2025) | Plug-and-play posterior sampling for fully blind inverse problems using dual diffusion model priors; currently leading on blind deblurring benchmarks arxiv | arxiv.org/abs/2505.22923 |\\n| **dasp-pytorch** | csteinmetz1 (independent/academic) | ✅ Active | Most comprehensive PyTorch library for differentiable audio processors with explicit blind estimation support github | github.com/csteinmetz1/dasp-pytorch |\\n| **Diff-MST** | Academic research project | ✅ Active (2023–present) | Full inverse mixing pipeline: given a reference mix, estimates all individual channel DSP parameters (EQ, compression, panning) using differentiable mixing console github | github.com/sai-soum/Diff-MST |\\n* * *\\n## 🗺️ Landscape Overview by Domain\\n| Domain | Maturity | Key Methods | Notable Gap |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| **Audio Effects (AFX)** | High | DDSP, neural proxies, auto-encoders | Limited work on nonlinear effects (saturation, distortion) inversion arxiv |\\n| **Room Acoustics** | High | Diffusion models, FDNs, MTL | Real-time blind RIR estimation remains challenging arxiv+1 |\\n| **Wireless Comms** | High | VAE, VQ-VAE, BW algorithm, CPA-Net | Joint channel + symbol rate + CFO estimation at very low SNR ieeexplore.ieee+1 |\\n| **Seismic** | Moderate | Sparse deconvolution, basis pursuit | Deep learning approaches underexplored compared to audio domain arxiv |\\n| **Medical Imaging** | Moderate | MAP estimation, diffusion priors | Instrument parameter co-estimation (semi-blind) still emerging arxiv+1 |\\n| **Optical Comms** | Emerging | VAE equalizers, butterfly filters | Nonlinear distortion + polarization combined blind estimation arxiv+1 |\\n* * *\\n## ⚠️ Identified Gaps & Emerging Directions\\n* **Causal, real-time inverse DSP**: Most methods are offline/batch; real-time blind parameter estimation for live audio processing remains underexplored.arxiv\\n* **Nonlinear effect inversion**: Blind estimation of saturation, distortion, and transient shaping is far less mature than linear effects like EQ and reverb.arxiv\\n* **Semi-blind (myopic) inversion**: Jointly estimating both the signal and _partial_ system parameters is now gaining traction (see arXiv:2602.11711 ) but lacks standardized benchmarks.arxiv\\n* **Cross-domain transfer**: Methods developed for audio AFX estimation are rarely adapted to seismic or radar blind deconvolution, despite shared mathematical structure.\\n* **Diffusion-prior blind inversion**: The Blind-PnPDM paradigm is very new (2025) and its applications outside image processing (e.g., to audio or comms) are not yet explored.arxiv\\n> **Especially Recommended for Your Work (Audio/AI Mastering context):**`dasp-pytorch`, the ICASSP 2024 BE-AFX paper, `Diff-MST`, and the 2024 DDSP review are the most directly applicable resources for building AI-powered blind DSP parameter estimation into music production tools.frontiersin+3\\nPrepared using Claude Sonnet 4.6 Thinking\\n45 sources\\nFollow-ups\\nComparison of variational Bayesian vs neural blind deconvolution\\nImplementation details of Diff-MST for audio signal restoration\\nSparse signal recovery applications in blind channel estimation\\nCurrent limitations of DDSP in real-time blind parameter estimation\\nKey open-source libraries for blind multichannel deconvolution\\nViewing a shared thread. Your follow-ups will be private to you.\\nAsk a follow-up\\nModel\\nSign in or create an account\\nSave and sync your searches\\nContinue with Google Continue with Apple\\nContinue with email\\nSingle sign-on (SSO)\\nInstant answers at your fingertips\\nBy continuing, you agree to our privacy policy.\\nContinue with Google Continue with Apple\\nContinue with email\\nSingle sign-on (SSO)\\nClose\"}"

EUSIPCO 2021; no labelled data required | Research release github | github.com/jundsp/VAE-BSS |\\n| **ssprl/Real-time-Blind-source-separation-using-IVA** | GitHub | Python/MATLAB | Real-time BSS via Independent Vector Analysis with NN-based sound source localization; optimized for hearing improvement applications | Published 2021 github | github.com/ssprl/Real-time-Blind-source-separation-using-IVA |\\n| **DavideNardone/Blind-Source-Separation-using-Dictionary-Learning** | GitHub | Python/MATLAB | BSS via sparse dictionary learning and mixing matrix estimation; block signal representation for computation efficiency | Archived (2017) github | github.com/DavideNardone/Blind-Source-Separation-using-Dictionary-Learning |\\n* * *\\n## 🔬 Active Projects & Frameworks\\n| Project | Institution / Org | Status | Description | Link |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| **DDSP (Differentiable DSP)** | Google Magenta | ✅ Active | Ongoing development of differentiable audio synthesis and processing primitives; widely adopted as the backbone of inverse audio DSP research github | magenta.github.io/ddsp |\\n| **BERP** | Academic (arXiv authors) | ✅ Active (2024–2025) | Universal framework for blind room parameter estimation; SOTA across acoustic and geometric parameters from single-channel recordings arxiv | arxiv.org/abs/2405.04476 |\\n| **BUDDy (Blind Unsupervised Dereverberation)** | Academic | ✅ Active (2024) | Score-based diffusion approach to joint blind dereverberation + RIR estimation; unsupervised and single-channel arxiv | arxiv.org/abs/2408.07472 |\\n| **Blind-PnPDM** | Academic | ✅ Active (2025) | Plug-and-play posterior sampling for fully blind inverse problems using dual diffusion model priors; currently leading on blind deblurring benchmarks arxiv | arxiv.org/abs/2505.22923 |\\n| **dasp-pytorch** | csteinmetz1 (independent/academic) | ✅ Active | Most comprehensive PyTorch library for differentiable audio processors with explicit blind estimation support github | github.com/csteinmetz1/dasp-pytorch |\\n| **Diff-MST** | Academic research project | ✅ Active (2023–present) | Full inverse mixing pipeline: given a reference mix, estimates all individual channel DSP parameters (EQ, compression, panning) using differentiable mixing console github | github.com/sai-soum/Diff-MST |\\n* * *\\n## 🗺️ Landscape Overview by Domain\\n| Domain | Maturity | Key Methods | Notable Gap |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| **Audio Effects (AFX)** | High | DDSP, neural proxies, auto-encoders | Limited work on nonlinear effects (saturation, distortion) inversion arxiv |\\n| **Room Acoustics** | High | Diffusion models, FDNs, MTL | Real-time blind RIR estimation remains challenging arxiv+1 |\\n| **Wireless Comms** | High | VAE, VQ-VAE, BW algorithm, CPA-Net | Joint channel + symbol rate + CFO estimation at very low SNR ieeexplore.ieee+1 |\\n| **Seismic** | Moderate | Sparse deconvolution, basis pursuit | Deep learning approaches underexplored compared to audio domain arxiv |\\n| **Medical Imaging** | Moderate | MAP estimation, diffusion priors | Instrument parameter co-estimation (semi-blind) still emerging arxiv+1 |\\n| **Optical Comms** | Emerging | VAE equalizers, butterfly filters | Nonlinear distortion + polarization combined blind estimation arxiv+1 |\\n* * *\\n## ⚠️ Identified Gaps & Emerging Directions\\n* **Causal, real-time inverse DSP**: Most methods are offline/batch; real-time blind parameter estimation for live audio processing remains underexplored.arxiv\\n* **Nonlinear effect inversion**: Blind estimation of saturation, distortion, and transient shaping is far less mature than linear effects like EQ and reverb.arxiv\\n* **Semi-blind (myopic) inversion**: Jointly estimating both the signal and _partial_ system parameters is now gaining traction (see arXiv:2602.11711 ) but lacks standardized benchmarks.arxiv\\n* **Cross-domain transfer**: Methods developed for audio AFX estimation are rarely adapted to seismic or radar blind deconvolution, despite shared mathematical structure.\\n* **Diffusion-prior blind inversion**: The Blind-PnPDM paradigm is very new (2025) and its applications outside image processing (e.g., to audio or comms) are not yet explored.arxiv\\n> **Especially Recommended for Your Work (Audio/AI Mastering context):**`dasp-pytorch`, the ICASSP 2024 BE-AFX paper, `Diff-MST`, and the 2024 DDSP review are the most directly applicable resources for building AI-powered blind DSP parameter estimation into music production tools.frontiersin+3\\nPrepared using Claude Sonnet 4.6 Thinking\\n45 sources\\nFollow-ups\\nComparison of variational Bayesian vs neural blind deconvolution\\nImplementation details of Diff-MST for audio signal restoration\\nSparse signal recovery applications in blind channel estimation\\nCurrent limitations of DDSP in real-time blind parameter estimation\\nKey open-source libraries for blind multichannel deconvolution\\nViewing a shared thread. content.content:
1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
4	
5	## Project Overview
6	
7	Audio-to-parameter regression system that reverse-engineers EQ/VST plugin settings from audio spectrograms. A ResNet18-based model ingests mel-spectrograms and predicts continuous parameter vectors (gains, frequencies, Q-factors for EQ bands, or arbitrary VST knob positions).
8	
9	## Commands
10	
11	### Training
12	```bash
13	# Primary training (PyTorch Lightning)
14	python train.py --audio_dir ./data --task_type vst --batch_size 16
15	# task_type choices: eq, vst, wer, synthetic
16	
17	# Production training script (standalone PyTorch, image classification)
18	python train_production.py --data_dir ./data --model resnet18 --epochs 50
19	```
20	
21	### Inference
22	```bash
23	python inference.py <audio_path> <checkpoint_path> --target_dim 24 --task_type generic
24	```
25	
26	### Evaluation
27	```bash
28	python evaluate_model.py --checkpoint <path.ckpt> --data_dir ./data --task_type vst
29	```
30	
31	### Data Generation & Preprocessing
32	```bash
33	# Generate synthetic EQ dataset (synthesizer.py)
34	python synthesizer.py  # generates 200 samples to ./synthetic_dataset
35	
36	# Precompute mel-spectrograms as .npy caches
37	python precompute_mels.py --data_dir ./data
38	
39	# Validate dataset integrity (JSON/audio pairing, dimension checks)
40	python validate_dataset.py --data_dir ./data --expected_dim 24
41	
42	# Audio quality screening pipeline
43	python audio_validator.py --input_dir ./raw --output_dir ./clean
44	
45	# VST plugin dataset generation via Pedalboard
46	python vst_generator.py --input_dir ./dry --output_dir ./rendered --vst "path/to/plugin.vst3"
47	
48	# Batch MorphSnap CLI integration
49	python batch_morphsnap.py --input_dir ./stems --output_dir ./output --vst "path/to/plugin.vst3"
50	
51	# TFRecord conversion for TensorFlow workflows
52	python create_tfrecords.py --data_dir ./data --output_dir ./tfrecords
53	```
54	
55	### 24-Band EQ Analysis (rule-based)
56	```bash
57	python eq_analyzer.py <audio_file>
58	```
59	
60	### Tests
61	```bash
62	python -m pytest tests/
63	# or
64	python -m unittest tests/test_normalization.py -v
65	```
66	
67	## Architecture
68	
69	### Core Pipeline: Audio → Mel-Spectrogram → ResNet18 → Parameter Vector
70	
71	**Model** (`model.py`): `GenericAudioRegressor` is a PyTorch Lightning module wrapping ResNet18 (modified for 1-channel input). It outputs a `target_dim`-sized vector. For `task_type='eq'`, the output is split into gains (tanh-scaled to +/-24dB), frequencies (log-scaled 20Hz-20kHz via sigmoid), Q-factors (sigmoid-scaled 0.1-10.0), and active probabilities (sigmoid). For other task types, raw linear output.
72	
73	**Loss** (`loss.py`): `ParameterMaskedLoss` applies MSE but masks frequency/Q loss when the target gain is near 0dB (<0.5dB threshold), preventing the model from fitting meaningless freq/Q values for inactive bands.
74	
75	**Datasets** (`dataset.py`): Hierarchy rooted at `BaseAudioDataset` which handles mel extraction (n_fft=4096, hop_length=1024, 128 mels, 20Hz-20kHz, CMVN normalization, optional StandardScaler, SpecAugment frequency masking). Four dataset classes:
76	- `VSTDataset` — pairs audio with `_config.json` sidecars containing `generative_components`
77	- `SyntheticDryDataset` — same sidecar format, with explicit band{N}_gain/freq/q parsing
78	- `EQAestheticDataset` — reads from a CSV manifest (`dataset_log.csv`) with per-band columns
79	- `WERDataset` — predicts a single WER score from `_wer.json` sidecars
80	
81	All datasets expect audio in `train/val/test` subdirectories with matching JSON sidecars.
82	
83	### Data Generation
84	
85	- `synthesizer.py` — Generates synthetic audio (harmonic complexes + colored noise) with parametric EQ applied via biquad filters. 30% boundary-case stratification (extreme gains/Q). Outputs FLAC + JSON sidecars with QC checks (NaN, clipping, crest factor, noise floor).
86	- `vst_generator.py` — Wraps real VST3 plugins via Pedalboard, rendering audio with automated parameter sweeps at 4 severity levels (clean/light/moderate/extreme).
87	- `batch_morphsnap.py` — Shell around an external `MorphSnapCLI.exe` that produces variations, then reshapes JSON output into the `generative_components` schema.
88	
89	### Supporting Utilities
90	
91	- `precompute_mels.py` — Parallel mel-spectrogram extraction to `.npy` files; `dataset.py` will load these instead of re-computing when present.
92	- `validate_dataset.py` — Multi-process validation that every audio file has a matching JSON sidecar with correct dimensionality and no NaN/Inf/outlier values.
93	- `audio_validator.py` — RMS/peak/ZCR-based audio quality screening.
94	- `eq_analyzer.py` — Standalone 24-band rule-based EQ analyzer (not ML-based); compares spectrum against pink noise reference.
95	- `create_tfrecords.py` — Converts the audio+JSON dataset into sharded TFRecords with a `tf.data` reader.
96	- `normalization/` — Standalone normalization utilities (`TrackedBatchNorm`, `TrackedLayerNorm`, `NumericalFeatureScaler`) with their own test suite.
97	
98	### Key Parameters
99	
100	- Mel extraction: n_fft=4096, hop_length=1024, n_mels=128, target_frames=128, sr=44100
101	- EQ bands: 8 by default (target_dim=24, i.e. 8 bands × 3 params: gain/freq/q)
102	- EQ parameter ranges: gain [-24, +24] dB, freq [20, 20000] Hz (log), Q [0.1, 10.0]
103	- Training: AdamW, early stopping (patience=5), TensorBoard logging to `tb_logs/`
104	
105	## Dependencies
106	
107	PyTorch, torchaudio, pytorch-lightning, librosa, soundfile, scikit-learn, pedalboard (for VST rendering), torchmetrics, scipy, tensorflow (only for TFRecord pipeline).
108

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

Audio-to-parameter regression system that reverse-engineers EQ/VST plugin settings from audio spectrograms. A ResNet18-based model ingests mel-spectrograms and predicts continuous parameter vectors (gains, frequencies, Q-factors for EQ bands, or arbitrary VST knob positions).

## Commands

### Training
```bash
# Primary training (PyTorch Lightning)
python train.py --audio_dir ./data --task_type vst --batch_size 16
# task_type choices: eq, vst, wer, synthetic

# Production training script (standalone PyTorch, image classification)
python train_production.py --data_dir ./data --model resnet18 --epochs 50
```

### Inference
```bash
python inference.py <audio_path> <checkpoint_path> --target_dim 24 --task_type generic
```

### Evaluation
```bash
python evaluate_model.py --checkpoint <path.ckpt> --data_dir ./data --task_type vst
```

### Data Generation & Preprocessing
```bash
# Generate synthetic EQ dataset (synthesizer.py)
python synthesizer.py  # generates 200 samples to ./synthetic_dataset

# Precompute mel-spectrograms as .npy caches
python precompute_mels.py --data_dir ./data

# Validate dataset integrity (JSON/audio pairing, dimension checks)
python validate_dataset.py --data_dir ./data --expected_dim 24

# Audio quality screening pipeline
python audio_validator.py --input_dir ./raw --output_dir ./clean

# VST plugin dataset generation via Pedalboard
python vst_generator.py --input_dir ./dry --output_dir ./rendered --vst "path/to/plugin.vst3"

# Batch MorphSnap CLI integration
python batch_morphsnap.py --input_dir ./stems --output_dir ./output --vst "path/to/plugin.vst3"

# TFRecord conversion for TensorFlow workflows
python create_tfrecords.py --data_dir ./data --output_dir ./tfrecords
```

### 24-Band EQ Analysis (rule-based)
```bash
python eq_analyzer.py <audio_file>
```

### Tests
```bash
python -m pytest tests/
# or
python -m unittest tests/test_normalization.py -v
```

## Architecture

### Core Pipeline: Audio → Mel-Spectrogram → ResNet18 → Parameter Vector

**Model** (`model.py`): `GenericAudioRegressor` is a PyTorch Lightning module wrapping ResNet18 (modified for 1-channel input). It outputs a `target_dim`-sized vector. For `task_type='eq'`, the output is split into gains (tanh-scaled to +/-24dB), frequencies (log-scaled 20Hz-20kHz via sigmoid), Q-factors (sigmoid-scaled 0.1-10.0), and active probabilities (sigmoid). For other task types, raw linear output.

**Loss** (`loss.py`): `ParameterMaskedLoss` applies MSE but masks frequency/Q loss when the target gain is near 0dB (<0.5dB threshold), preventing the model from fitting meaningless freq/Q values for inactive bands.

**Datasets** (`dataset.py`): Hierarchy rooted at `BaseAudioDataset` which handles mel extraction (n_fft=4096, hop_length=1024, 128 mels, 20Hz-20kHz, CMVN normalization, optional StandardScaler, SpecAugment frequency masking). Four dataset classes:
- `VSTDataset` — pairs audio with `_config.json` sidecars containing `generative_components`
- `SyntheticDryDataset` — same sidecar format, with explicit band{N}_gain/freq/q parsing
- `EQAestheticDataset` — reads from a CSV manifest (`dataset_log.csv`) with per-band columns
- `WERDataset` — predicts a single WER score from `_wer.json` sidecars

All datasets expect audio in `train/val/test` subdirectories with matching JSON sidecars.

### Data Generation

- `synthesizer.py` — Generates synthetic audio (harmonic complexes + colored noise) with parametric EQ applied via biquad filters. 30% boundary-case stratification (extreme gains/Q). Outputs FLAC + JSON sidecars with QC checks (NaN, clipping, crest factor, noise floor).
- `vst_generator.py` — Wraps real VST3 plugins via Pedalboard, rendering audio with automated parameter sweeps at 4 severity levels (clean/light/moderate/extreme).
- `batch_morphsnap.py` — Shell around an external `MorphSnapCLI.exe` that produces variations, then reshapes JSON output into the `generative_components` schema.

### Supporting Utilities

- `precompute_mels.py` — Parallel mel-spectrogram extraction to `.npy` files; `dataset.py` will load these instead of re-computing when present.
- `validate_dataset.py` — Multi-process validation that every audio file has a matching JSON sidecar with correct dimensionality and no NaN/Inf/outlier values.
- `audio_validator.py` — RMS/peak/ZCR-based audio quality screening.
- `eq_analyzer.py` — Standalone 24-band rule-based EQ analyzer (not ML-based); compares spectrum against pink noise reference.
- `create_tfrecords.py` — Converts the audio+JSON dataset into sharded TFRecords with a `tf.data` reader.
- `normalization/` — Standalone normalization utilities (`TrackedBatchNorm`, `TrackedLayerNorm`, `NumericalFeatureScaler`) with their own test suite.

### Key Parameters

- Mel extraction: n_fft=4096, hop_length=1024, n_mels=128, target_frames=128, sr=44100
- EQ bands: 8 by default (target_dim=24, i.e. 8 bands × 3 params: gain/freq/q)
- EQ parameter ranges: gain [-24, +24] dB, freq [20, 20000] Hz (log), Q [0.1, 10.0]
- Training: AdamW, early stopping (patience=5), TensorBoard logging to `tb_logs/`

## Dependencies

PyTorch, torchaudio, pytorch-lightning, librosa, soundfile, scikit-learn, pedalboard (for VST rendering), torchmetrics, scipy, tensorflow (only for TFRecord pipeline).

content.content:
1	import math
2	import torch
3	import torch.nn as nn
4	import torch.nn.functional as F
5	import pytorch_lightning as pl

8	class SqueezeExcitation(nn.Module):
9	    """Channel attention: learns which feature channels matter."""
10	    def __init__(self, channels: int, reduction: int = 16):
11	        super().__init__()
12	        mid = max(channels // reduction, 8)
13	        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1)
14	        self.fc = nn.Sequential(
15	            nn.Linear(channels, mid, bias=False),
16	            nn.SiLU(inplace=True),
17	            nn.Linear(mid, channels, bias=False),
18	            nn.Sigmoid(),
19	        )
20	
21	    def forward(self, x):
22	        b, c, _, _ = x.shape
23	        w = self.fc(self.pool(x).view(b, c))
24	        return x * w.view(b, c, 1, 1)

27	def normalize_params(y: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
28	    """
29	    Normalizes a tensor of shape (..., 24) to [0, 1].
30	    Expected order: [f1, g1, q1, f2, g2, q2, ... f8, g8, q8]
31	    """
32	    y_norm = torch.zeros_like(y)
33	
34	    # 1. Frequencies: Logarithmic Min-Max Scaling (20Hz to 20kHz)
35	    freqs = y[..., 0::3]
36	    f_min, f_max = 20.0, 20000.0
37	    freqs_log = torch.log10(torch.clamp(freqs, min=f_min, max=f_max))
38	    log_min, log_max = torch.log10(torch.tensor(f_min)), torch.log10(torch.tensor(f_max))
39	    y_norm[..., 0::3] = (freqs_log - log_min) / (log_max - log_min)
40	
41	    # 2. Gains: Linear Min-Max Scaling (-12 to +12 dB)
42	    gains = y[..., 1::3]
43	    g_min, g_max = -12.0, 12.0
44	    y_norm[..., 1::3] = (torch.clamp(gains, min=g_min, max=g_max) - g_min) / (g_max - g_min)
45	
46	    # 3. Q-Factors: Logarithmic Min-Max Scaling (0.5 to 4.0) — matches training data range
47	    qs = y[..., 2::3]
48	    q_min, q_max = 0.5, 4.0
49	    qs_log = torch.log10(torch.clamp(qs, min=q_min, max=q_max))
50	    log_q_min = torch.log10(torch.tensor(q_min))
51	    log_q_max = torch.log10(torch.tensor(q_max))
52	    y_norm[..., 2::3] = (qs_log - log_q_min) / (log_q_max - log_q_min)
53	
54	    return y_norm
55	
56	
57	def denormalize_params(y_norm: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
58	    """
59	    Reverses the normalization to return real-world parameters.
60	    """
61	    y_real = torch.zeros_like(y_norm)
62	
63	    # Frequencies
64	    freqs_norm = y_norm[..., 0::3]
65	    f_min, f_max = 20.0, 20000.0
66	    log_min, log_max = torch.log10(torch.tensor(f_min)), torch.log10(torch.tensor(f_max))
67	    y_real[..., 0::3] = 10 ** (freqs_norm * (log_max - log_min) + log_min)
68	
69	    # Gains
70	    gains_norm = y_norm[..., 1::3]
71	    g_min, g_max = -12.0, 12.0
72	    y_real[..., 1::3] = gains_norm * (g_max - g_min) + g_min
73	
74	    # Q-Factors
75	    qs_norm = y_norm[..., 2::3]
76	    q_min, q_max = 0.5, 4.0
77	    log_q_min = torch.log10(torch.tensor(q_min))
78	    log_q_max = torch.log10(torch.tensor(q_max))
79	    y_real[..., 2::3] = 10 ** (qs_norm * (log_q_max - log_q_min) + log_q_min)
80	
81	    return y_real

84	class EQRegressor(pl.LightningModule):
85	    def __init__(self, lr: float = 5e-4, warmup_epochs: int = 5, max_epochs: int = 100):
86	        super().__init__()
87	        self.save_hyperparameters()
88	        self._lr = lr
89	        self._warmup_epochs = warmup_epochs
90	        self._max_epochs = max_epochs
91	
92	        # 1. Convolutional Feature Extractor with SE attention
93	        #    5 blocks: 3→32→64→128→256→512, each with MaxPool2d(2).
94	        #    SE blocks after blocks 3, 4, 5 let the network learn which
95	        #    frequency channels matter for each EQ band.
96	        self.block1 = nn.Sequential(
97	            nn.Conv2d(3, 32, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(32), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
98	        )
99	        self.block2 = nn.Sequential(
100	            nn.Conv2d(32, 64, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(64), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
101	        )
102	        self.block3 = nn.Sequential(
103	            nn.Conv2d(64, 128, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(128), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
104	        )
105	        self.se3 = SqueezeExcitation(128, reduction=16)
106	        self.block4 = nn.Sequential(
107	            nn.Conv2d(128, 256, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(256), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
108	        )
109	        self.se4 = SqueezeExcitation(256, reduction=16)
110	        self.block5 = nn.Sequential(
111	            nn.Conv2d(256, 512, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(512), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
112	        )
113	        self.se5 = SqueezeExcitation(512, reduction=16)
114	
115	        # 2. Spatial pooling preserves 4×4 grid → 512*16 = 8,192 features.
116	        #    GAP(1,1) collapsed ALL spatial information to a single scalar per
117	        #    channel, destroying the frequency-localization the regressor needs.
118	        #    GAP((4,4)) keeps a coarse time-frequency grid so the FC head can
119	        #    learn which spectral regions correspond to which EQ band.
120	        self.spatial_pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((4, 4))
121	        self.cnn_spatial_dim = 512 * 4 * 4  # 8,192
122	
123	        # 3. Amplitude Bypass Branch — average-pools the diff channel (ch 2)
124	        #    preserving per-mel-bin energy.  AvgPool (not MaxPool) gives a more
125	        #    stable representation that doesn't amplify transient spikes.
126	        self.amp_pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((128, 1))
127	        self.amp_flat_dim = 128
128	
129	        self.flatten_dim = self.cnn_spatial_dim + self.amp_flat_dim  # 8,320
130	
131	        # 4. FC Head with reduced dropout (model is underfitting, not overfitting).
132	        #    SiLU activations for smoother gradients than ReLU.
133	        self.fc = nn.Sequential(
134	            nn.Linear(self.flatten_dim, 512),
135	            nn.BatchNorm1d(512),
136	            nn.SiLU(),
137	            nn.Dropout(0.1),
138	            nn.Linear(512, 256),
139	            nn.SiLU(),
140	            nn.Dropout(0.05),
141	            nn.Linear(256, 24),
142	            nn.Sigmoid(),
143	        )
144	
145	    def forward(self, x):
146	        # Save original input for amplitude bypass BEFORE CNN processing
147	        orig_input = x
148	
149	        # CNN blocks with SE attention after deeper layers
150	        x = self.block1(x)
151	        x = self.block2(x)
152	        x = self.se3(self.block3(x))
153	        x = self.se4(self.block4(x))
154	        x = self.se5(self.block5(x))
155	
156	        # Spatial pool preserves coarse frequency-time grid
157	        cnn_flat = self.spatial_pool(x).flatten(1)   # [B, 8192]
158	
159	        # Amplitude bypass: average-pool the ORIGINAL diff channel (ch 2).
160	        # Must use orig_input, not CNN output, to get actual spectral delta.
161	        diff = orig_input[:, 2:3, :, :]              # [B, 1, 128, 431]
162	        amp_flat = self.amp_pool(diff).flatten(1)     # [B, 128]
163	
164	        combined = torch.cat([cnn_flat, amp_flat], dim=1)  # [B, 8320]
165	        return self.fc(combined)
166	
167	    def _compute_loss(self, y_hat_norm, y_norm):
168	        # gain_weight: distance from unity (0.5) with a floor of 0.1 so
169	        # freq/Q always receive gradient signal, even for moderate gains.
170	        gain_weight = torch.clamp(torch.abs(y_norm[:, 1::3] - 0.5) * 2.0, min=0.1)
171	
172	        # SmoothL1 with β=0.05 — since predictions & labels are in [0,1],
173	        # the default β=1.0 makes SmoothL1 identical to MSE.  Setting β=0.05
174	        # creates a quadratic→linear transition for errors > 0.05, reducing
175	        # the influence of outlier predictions and accelerating convergence.
176	        beta = 0.05
177	        loss_g = F.smooth_l1_loss(y_hat_norm[:, 1::3], y_norm[:, 1::3],
178	                                  reduction='mean', beta=beta)
179	        loss_f = (F.smooth_l1_loss(y_hat_norm[:, 0::3], y_norm[:, 0::3],
180	                                   reduction='none', beta=beta) * gain_weight).mean()
181	        loss_q = (F.smooth_l1_loss(y_hat_norm[:, 2::3], y_norm[:, 2::3],
182	                                   reduction='none', beta=beta) * gain_weight).mean()
183	        return 1.0 * loss_g + 0.5 * loss_f + 0.5 * loss_q, loss_g, loss_f, loss_q
184	
185	    def training_step(self, batch, batch_idx):
186	        x, y = batch
187	        y_hat_norm = self(x)
188	        loss, loss_g, loss_f, loss_q = self._compute_loss(y_hat_norm, y)
189	        self.log('train_loss',   loss,   prog_bar=False)
190	        self.log('train_loss_g', loss_g)
191	        self.log('train_loss_f', loss_f)
192	        self.log('train_loss_q', loss_q)
193	        return loss
194	
195	    def validation_step(self, batch, batch_idx):
196	        x, y = batch
197	        y_hat_norm = self(x)
198	        loss, loss_g, loss_f, loss_q = self._compute_loss(y_hat_norm, y)
199	        self.log('val_loss',   loss,   prog_bar=True)
200	        self.log('val_loss_g', loss_g)
201	        self.log('val_loss_f', loss_f)
202	        self.log('val_loss_q', loss_q)
203	        return loss
204	
205	    def _get_lr(self):
206	        # Returns a MULTIPLIER (0-1) for LambdaLR, not an absolute LR.
207	        # LambdaLR computes: effective_lr = initial_lr × this_value
208	        epoch = self.current_epoch
209	        if epoch < self._warmup_epochs:
210	            return (epoch + 1) / self._warmup_epochs
211	        progress = (epoch - self._warmup_epochs) / max(1, self._max_epochs - self._warmup_epochs)
212	        return 0.5 * (1.0 + math.cos(math.pi * progress))
213	
214	    def configure_optimizers(self):
215	        # Separate weight decay: apply to weights only, not biases/BN params
216	        decay_params = []
217	        no_decay_params = []
218	        for name, param in self.named_parameters():
219	            if not param.requires_grad:
220	                continue
221	            if param.dim() >= 2:
222	                decay_params.append(param)
223	            else:
224	                no_decay_params.append(param)
225	
226	        param_groups = [
227	            {"params": decay_params,    "weight_decay": 1e-4},
228	            {"params": no_decay_params, "weight_decay": 0.0},
229	        ]
230	        optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(param_groups, lr=self._lr)
231	        scheduler = torch.optim.lr_scheduler.LambdaLR(
232	            optimizer, lr_lambda=lambda _: self._get_lr()
233	        )
234	        return {
235	            "optimizer": optimizer,
236	            "lr_scheduler": {
237	                "scheduler": scheduler,
238	                "interval": "epoch",
239	                "frequency": 1,
240	            },
241	        }
242

import math
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import pytorch_lightning as pl


class SqueezeExcitation(nn.Module):
    """Channel attention: learns which feature channels matter."""
    def __init__(self, channels: int, reduction: int = 16):
        super().__init__()
        mid = max(channels // reduction, 8)
        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1)
        self.fc = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(channels, mid, bias=False),
            nn.SiLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Linear(mid, channels, bias=False),
            nn.Sigmoid(),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        b, c, _, _ = x.shape
        w = self.fc(self.pool(x).view(b, c))
        return x * w.view(b, c, 1, 1)


def normalize_params(y: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Normalizes a tensor of shape (..., 24) to [0, 1].
    Expected order: [f1, g1, q1, f2, g2, q2, ... f8, g8, q8]
    """
    y_norm = torch.zeros_like(y)

    # 1. Frequencies: Logarithmic Min-Max Scaling (20Hz to 20kHz)
    freqs = y[..., 0::3]
    f_min, f_max = 20.0, 20000.0
    freqs_log = torch.log10(torch.clamp(freqs, min=f_min, max=f_max))
    log_min, log_max = torch.log10(torch.tensor(f_min)), torch.log10(torch.tensor(f_max))
    y_norm[..., 0::3] = (freqs_log - log_min) / (log_max - log_min)

    # 2. Gains: Linear Min-Max Scaling (-12 to +12 dB)
    gains = y[..., 1::3]
    g_min, g_max = -12.0, 12.0
    y_norm[..., 1::3] = (torch.clamp(gains, min=g_min, max=g_max) - g_min) / (g_max - g_min)

    # 3. Q-Factors: Logarithmic Min-Max Scaling (0.5 to 4.0) — matches training data range
    qs = y[..., 2::3]
    q_min, q_max = 0.5, 4.0
    qs_log = torch.log10(torch.clamp(qs, min=q_min, max=q_max))
    log_q_min = torch.log10(torch.tensor(q_min))
    log_q_max = torch.log10(torch.tensor(q_max))
    y_norm[..., 2::3] = (qs_log - log_q_min) / (log_q_max - log_q_min)

    return y_norm


def denormalize_params(y_norm: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Reverses the normalization to return real-world parameters.
    """
    y_real = torch.zeros_like(y_norm)

    # Frequencies
    freqs_norm = y_norm[..., 0::3]
    f_min, f_max = 20.0, 20000.0
    log_min, log_max = torch.log10(torch.tensor(f_min)), torch.log10(torch.tensor(f_max))
    y_real[..., 0::3] = 10 ** (freqs_norm * (log_max - log_min) + log_min)

    # Gains
    gains_norm = y_norm[..., 1::3]
    g_min, g_max = -12.0, 12.0
    y_real[..., 1::3] = gains_norm * (g_max - g_min) + g_min

    # Q-Factors
    qs_norm = y_norm[..., 2::3]
    q_min, q_max = 0.5, 4.0
    log_q_min = torch.log10(torch.tensor(q_min))
    log_q_max = torch.log10(torch.tensor(q_max))
    y_real[..., 2::3] = 10 ** (qs_norm * (log_q_max - log_q_min) + log_q_min)

    return y_real


class EQRegressor(pl.LightningModule):
    def __init__(self, lr: float = 5e-4, warmup_epochs: int = 5, max_epochs: int = 100):
        super().__init__()
        self.save_hyperparameters()
        self._lr = lr
        self._warmup_epochs = warmup_epochs
        self._max_epochs = max_epochs

        # 1. Convolutional Feature Extractor with SE attention
        #    5 blocks: 3→32→64→128→256→512, each with MaxPool2d(2).
        #    SE blocks after blocks 3, 4, 5 let the network learn which
        #    frequency channels matter for each EQ band.
        self.block1 = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(3, 32, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(32), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
        )
        self.block2 = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(32, 64, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(64), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
        )
        self.block3 = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(64, 128, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(128), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
        )
        self.se3 = SqueezeExcitation(128, reduction=16)
        self.block4 = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(128, 256, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(256), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
        )
        self.se4 = SqueezeExcitation(256, reduction=16)
        self.block5 = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(256, 512, 3, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(512), nn.SiLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
        )
        self.se5 = SqueezeExcitation(512, reduction=16)

        # 2. Spatial pooling preserves 4×4 grid → 512*16 = 8,192 features.
        #    GAP(1,1) collapsed ALL spatial information to a single scalar per
        #    channel, destroying the frequency-localization the regressor needs.
        #    GAP((4,4)) keeps a coarse time-frequency grid so the FC head can
        #    learn which spectral regions correspond to which EQ band.
        self.spatial_pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((4, 4))
        self.cnn_spatial_dim = 512 * 4 * 4  # 8,192

        # 3. Amplitude Bypass Branch — average-pools the diff channel (ch 2)
        #    preserving per-mel-bin energy.  AvgPool (not MaxPool) gives a more
        #    stable representation that doesn't amplify transient spikes.
        self.amp_pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((128, 1))
        self.amp_flat_dim = 128

        self.flatten_dim = self.cnn_spatial_dim + self.amp_flat_dim  # 8,320

        # 4. FC Head with reduced dropout (model is underfitting, not overfitting).
        #    SiLU activations for smoother gradients than ReLU.
        self.fc = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(self.flatten_dim, 512),
            nn.BatchNorm1d(512),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.1),
            nn.Linear(512, 256),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.05),
            nn.Linear(256, 24),
            nn.Sigmoid(),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        # Save original input for amplitude bypass BEFORE CNN processing
        orig_input = x

        # CNN blocks with SE attention after deeper layers
        x = self.block1(x)
        x = self.block2(x)
        x = self.se3(self.block3(x))
        x = self.se4(self.block4(x))
        x = self.se5(self.block5(x))

        # Spatial pool preserves coarse frequency-time grid
        cnn_flat = self.spatial_pool(x).flatten(1)   # [B, 8192]

        # Amplitude bypass: average-pool the ORIGINAL diff channel (ch 2).
        # Must use orig_input, not CNN output, to get actual spectral delta.
        diff = orig_input[:, 2:3, :, :]              # [B, 1, 128, 431]
        amp_flat = self.amp_pool(diff).flatten(1)     # [B, 128]

        combined = torch.cat([cnn_flat, amp_flat], dim=1)  # [B, 8320]
        return self.fc(combined)

    def _compute_loss(self, y_hat_norm, y_norm):
        # gain_weight: distance from unity (0.5) with a floor of 0.1 so
        # freq/Q always receive gradient signal, even for moderate gains.
        gain_weight = torch.clamp(torch.abs(y_norm[:, 1::3] - 0.5) * 2.0, min=0.1)

        # SmoothL1 with β=0.05 — since predictions & labels are in [0,1],
        # the default β=1.0 makes SmoothL1 identical to MSE.  Setting β=0.05
        # creates a quadratic→linear transition for errors > 0.05, reducing
        # the influence of outlier predictions and accelerating convergence.
        beta = 0.05
        loss_g = F.smooth_l1_loss(y_hat_norm[:, 1::3], y_norm[:, 1::3],
                                  reduction='mean', beta=beta)
        loss_f = (F.smooth_l1_loss(y_hat_norm[:, 0::3], y_norm[:, 0::3],
                                   reduction='none', beta=beta) * gain_weight).mean()
        loss_q = (F.smooth_l1_loss(y_hat_norm[:, 2::3], y_norm[:, 2::3],
                                   reduction='none', beta=beta) * gain_weight).mean()
        return 1.0 * loss_g + 0.5 * loss_f + 0.5 * loss_q, loss_g, loss_f, loss_q

    def training_step(self, batch, batch_idx):
        x, y = batch
        y_hat_norm = self(x)
        loss, loss_g, loss_f, loss_q = self._compute_loss(y_hat_norm, y)
        self.log('train_loss',   loss,   prog_bar=False)
        self.log('train_loss_g', loss_g)
        self.log('train_loss_f', loss_f)
        self.log('train_loss_q', loss_q)
        return loss

    def validation_step(self, batch, batch_idx):
        x, y = batch
        y_hat_norm = self(x)
        loss, loss_g, loss_f, loss_q = self._compute_loss(y_hat_norm, y)
        self.log('val_loss',   loss,   prog_bar=True)
        self.log('val_loss_g', loss_g)
        self.log('val_loss_f', loss_f)
        self.log('val_loss_q', loss_q)
        return loss

    def _get_lr(self):
        # Returns a MULTIPLIER (0-1) for LambdaLR, not an absolute LR.
        # LambdaLR computes: effective_lr = initial_lr × this_value
        epoch = self.current_epoch
        if epoch < self._warmup_epochs:
            return (epoch + 1) / self._warmup_epochs
        progress = (epoch - self._warmup_epochs) / max(1, self._max_epochs - self._warmup_epochs)
        return 0.5 * (1.0 + math.cos(math.pi * progress))

    def configure_optimizers(self):
        # Separate weight decay: apply to weights only, not biases/BN params
        decay_params = []
        no_decay_params = []
        for name, param in self.named_parameters():
            if not param.requires_grad:
                continue
            if param.dim() >= 2:
                decay_params.append(param)
            else:
                no_decay_params.append(param)

        param_groups = [
            {"params": decay_params,    "weight_decay": 1e-4},
            {"params": no_decay_params, "weight_decay": 0.0},
        ]
        optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(param_groups, lr=self._lr)
        scheduler = torch.optim.lr_scheduler.LambdaLR(
            optimizer, lr_lambda=lambda _: self._get_lr()
        )
        return {
            "optimizer": optimizer,
            "lr_scheduler": {
                "scheduler": scheduler,
                "interval": "epoch",
                "frequency": 1,
            },
        }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Test the Normalization Implementation
    print("Testing Parameter Normalization...")
    
    # Create the test dummy tensor provided by User
    dummy_input = torch.tensor([[1083.3, 11.98, 3.398]])
    print(f"Original Input:\n  Frequency: {dummy_input[0,0]:.1f} Hz\n  Gain: {dummy_input[0,1]:.2f} dB\n  Q: {dummy_input[0,2]:.3f}")
    
    normalized = normalize_params(dummy_input)
    print(f"\nNormalized Output (Expected range [0, 1]):\n  Frequency: {normalized[0,0]:.4f}\n  Gain: {normalized[0,1]:.4f}\n  Q: {normalized[0,2]:.4f}")
    
    denormalized = denormalize_params(normalized)
    print(f"\nDenormalized Validation (Should match Original):\n  Frequency: {denormalized[0,0]:.1f} Hz\n  Gain: {denormalized[0,1]:.2f} dB\n  Q: {denormalized[0,2]:.3f}")

    print("\n" + "=" * 50)
    print("Testing EQRegressor Architecture...")
    model = EQRegressor()
    dummy_input = torch.randn(1, 3, 128, 431)
    print(f"Input shape: {dummy_input.shape}")
    print(f"flatten_dim = {model.flatten_dim} "
          f"(cnn_spatial={model.cnn_spatial_dim} + amp={model.amp_flat_dim})")

    output = model(dummy_input)
    print(f"Output shape: {output.shape} (Expected: (1, 24))")

    total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
    print(f"Total parameters: {total_params:,}")
    print("=" * 50)

file.content:
# EQ Param

Reverse-engineer EQ and VST plugin parameters from audio. Feed in a processed track, get back the gain, frequency, and Q values of each EQ band.

## How It Works

```
Audio → Mel-Spectrogram → ResNet18 → [gain, freq, Q] per band
```

A ResNet18 backbone (adapted for single-channel mel-spectrograms) learns to map spectral features to continuous parameter vectors. For EQ tasks it predicts up to 8 bands of parameters along with band active probabilities (24 outputs). For generic VST tasks it predicts arbitrary knob positions.

## Quick Start

### Install

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

Requires: PyTorch, torchaudio, pytorch-lightning, librosa, soundfile, scikit-learn, torchmetrics, scipy. Optional: `pedalboard` for VST rendering, `tensorflow` for TFRecord pipeline.

### 1. Generate Training Data

```bash
# Synthetic dataset (fastest way to get started)
python synthesizer.py
```

This creates 200 audio+JSON pairs in `./synthetic_dataset/` split 80/10/10 into train/val/test.

### 2. Train

```bash
python train.py --audio_dir ./synthetic_dataset --task_type eq --batch_size 16
```

Checkpoints save to `checkpoints/`. TensorBoard logs to `tb_logs/`.

### 3. Predict Parameters

```bash
python inference.py path/to/audio.wav checkpoints/eq-best.ckpt --task_type eq
```

Output example:
```
=== EQ PARAMETER EXTRACTION ===
Band 0:
  Gain: -12.34 dB
  Freq: 440.0 Hz
  Q:    1.50
  Active Prob: 0.999
Band 1:
  Gain: 6.21 dB
  Freq: 3200.0 Hz
  Q:    2.80
  Active Prob: 0.842
...
```

### 4. Evaluate

```bash
python evaluate_model.py --checkpoint checkpoints/eq-best.ckpt --data_dir ./synthetic_dataset --task_type eq
```

Outputs R2, RMSE, MAE metrics and a truth-vs-prediction scatter plot.

## Dataset Sources

| Source | Script | Description |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| Synthetic | `synthesizer.py` | Harmonic+noise audio with random parametric EQ, 30% boundary-case stratification |
| VST Plugin | `vst_generator.py` | Renders dry audio through real VST3 plugins via Pedalboard |
| MorphSnap | `batch_morphsnap.py` | Batch wrapper around MorphSnapCLI for VST parameter exploration |
| Custom | Manual | Place audio+JSON sidecars in `train/`/`val/`/`test/` directories |

### Data Format

Each audio file needs a matching JSON sidecar:

```
data/train/wet/sample_42_wet.flac
data/train/wet/sample_42_config.json
```

JSON structure:
```json
{
  "generative_components": {
    "band0_active": true,
    "band0_gain": -12.0,
    "band0_freq": 440.0,
    "band0_q": 1.5,
    "band1_active": false,
    "band1_gain": 0.0,
    "band1_freq": 3200.0,
    "band1_q": 2.8
  }
}
```

### Preprocessing

```bash
# Cache mel-spectrograms as .npy (speeds up training)
python precompute_mels.py --data_dir ./data

# Validate dataset integrity before training
python validate_dataset.py --data_dir ./data --expected_dim 24

# Screen raw audio for quality issues
python audio_validator.py --input_dir ./raw --output_dir ./clean
```

## Task Types

| Task | `--task_type` | Output | Target Dim |
|------|---------------|--------|------------|
| EQ Regression | `eq` | gains, freqs, Q, active_probs | 24 (8 bands x 3) |
| Generic VST | `vst` | Raw parameter vector | Dynamic |
| WER Score | `wer` | Single float | 1 |
| Synthetic | `synthetic` | Raw parameter vector | Dynamic |

## Additional Tools

- **`eq_analyzer.py`** - Rule-based 24-band EQ analysis (not ML). Compares audio spectrum against a pink noise reference and suggests corrective EQ.
- **`create_tfrecords.py`** - Converts datasets to sharded TFRecords for TensorFlow pipelines.
- **`train_production.py`** - Standalone PyTorch training script for image classification tasks (separate from the audio pipeline).

## Configuration

Key mel-spectrogram parameters (hardcoded in `dataset.py`):

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Sample rate | 44100 Hz | |
| n_fft | 4096 | High frequency resolution |
| Hop length | 1024 | |
| n_mels | 128 | |
| Target frames | 128 | Fixed width for CNN input |
| Frequency range | 20–20000 Hz | |

EQ parameter ranges (bounded by the model's output activations):

| Parameter | Range | Activation |
|-----------|-------|------------|
| Gain | -24 to +24 dB | tanh |
| Frequency | 20 to 20000 Hz | sigmoid (log scale) |
| Q | 0.1 to 10.0 | sigmoid |

1	import os
2	import random
3	import torch
4	from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader, random_split, Subset
5	import pytorch_lightning as pl
6	import numpy as np
7	from pathlib import Path
8	
9	# Import normalization from model so labels are always in [0,1]
10	from model_cnn import normalize_params
11	
12	
13	class EQDataset(Dataset):
14	    """
15	    Dataset for loading synthetic EQ `.npz` files.
16	    Supports two schema formats:
17	      Format A (pre-processed):  keys 'mel_stack' [3,128,431] + 'label' [24] (already normalized)
18	      Format B (raw generator):  keys 'dry_mel' [128,431] + 'wet_mel' [128,431] + 'labels' [24] (raw Hz/dB/Q)
19	    """
20	    def __init__(self, data_dir: str | Path, training_mode: bool = False,
21	                 cache_in_ram: bool = False, global_norm: tuple | None = None):
22	        super().__init__()
23	        self.data_dir = Path(data_dir)
24	        self.training_mode = training_mode
25	        self.cache_in_ram = cache_in_ram
26	        self.files = sorted(self.data_dir.rglob("*.npz"))
27	        if not self.files:
28	            raise FileNotFoundError(f"No .npz files found in {self.data_dir}")
29	
30	        # Detect schema from first file
31	        sample = np.load(self.files[0])
32	        self._has_mel_stack = "mel_stack" in sample
33	        self._label_key = "label" if "label" in sample else "labels"
34	
35	        # Global normalization stats: (mean[3], std[3]) per channel
36	        self._global_mean = None
37	        self._global_std = None
38	        if global_norm is not None:
39	            self._global_mean = torch.tensor(global_norm[0], dtype=torch.float32).view(3, 1, 1)
40	            self._global_std = torch.tensor(global_norm[1], dtype=torch.float32).view(3, 1, 1)
41	
42	        if self.cache_in_ram:
43	            self._cache = [np.load(f) for f in self.files]
44	
45	    def __len__(self) -> int:
46	        return len(self.files)
47	
48	    def _build_mel_stack(self, data: np.lib.npyio.NpzFile) -> np.ndarray:
49	        """Build 3-channel mel_stack [3, 128, 431] from whatever keys are present."""
50	        if self._has_mel_stack:
51	            return data["mel_stack"]
52	
53	        # Format B: reconstruct from dry_mel + wet_mel
54	        dry = data["dry_mel"]   # [128, 431]
55	        wet = data["wet_mel"]   # [128, 431]
56	        diff = wet - dry
57	        return np.stack([wet, dry, diff], axis=0).astype(np.float32)
58	
59	    def __getitem__(self, idx: int) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
60	        if self.cache_in_ram:
61	            data = self._cache[idx]
62	        else:
63	            data = np.load(self.files[idx])
64	
65	        x = torch.tensor(self._build_mel_stack(data), dtype=torch.float32)
66	        y = torch.tensor(data[self._label_key], dtype=torch.float32)
67	
68	        # Normalize raw labels to [0,1] so Sigmoid output can match.
69	        # Detect raw vs pre-normalized: raw labels have values > 1.0 (e.g., freq in Hz).
70	        # Pre-normalized datasets already have labels in [0,1] — skip to avoid
71	        # double-normalization which would compress the target range.
72	        if y.max() > 1.5:
73	            y = normalize_params(y)
74	
75	        # Global normalization of mel-spectrogram input
76	        if self._global_mean is not None:
77	            x = (x - self._global_mean) / self._global_std.clamp(min=1e-6)
78	
79	        # Training augmentation: frequency masking (SpecAugment-style)
80	        if self.training_mode:
81	            n_mel_bins = x.shape[1]
82	            for _ in range(random.randint(1, 3)):
83	                f_start = random.randint(0, n_mel_bins - 1)
84	                f_width = random.randint(1, min(8, n_mel_bins - f_start))
85	                x[:, f_start:f_start + f_width, :] = 0.0
86	
87	        return x, y
88	
89	
90	class EQDataModule(pl.LightningDataModule):
91	    """
92	    Lightning DataModule mapping the EQDataset into Train and Val loaders
93	    with standard 80/20 splits.
94	    """
95	    def __init__(self, data_dir: str | Path, batch_size: int = 128, num_workers: int = 8):
96	        super().__init__()
97	        self.data_dir = data_dir
98	        self.batch_size = batch_size
99	        self.num_workers = num_workers
100	
101	        # These will be populated in setup()
102	        self.train_dataset = None
103	        self.val_dataset = None
104	        self._global_norm = None
105	
106	    def setup(self, stage: str = None):
107	        """Instantiates the dataset and creates the 80/20 split."""
108	        full_dataset = EQDataset(self.data_dir)
109	
110	        train_size = int(0.8 * len(full_dataset))
111	        val_size = len(full_dataset) - train_size
112	
113	        # Get reproducible indices for the split (same seed as before)
114	        generator = torch.Generator().manual_seed(42)
115	        indices = torch.randperm(len(full_dataset), generator=generator).tolist()
116	        train_indices = indices[:train_size]
117	        val_indices = indices[train_size:]
118	
119	        # Compute global normalization statistics from training data ONLY.
120	        sample_indices = random.Random(42).sample(train_indices, min(500, len(train_indices)))
121	        channel_sums = np.zeros(3, dtype=np.float64)
122	        channel_sq_sums = np.zeros(3, dtype=np.float64)
123	        n_pixels = 0
124	        for idx in sample_indices:
125	            data = np.load(full_dataset.files[idx])
126	            mel = full_dataset._build_mel_stack(data).astype(np.float64)
127	            for c in range(3):
128	                channel_sums[c] += mel[c].sum()
129	                channel_sq_sums[c] += (mel[c] ** 2).sum()
130	            n_pixels += mel.shape[1] * mel.shape[2]
131	
132	        means = channel_sums / n_pixels
133	        stds = np.sqrt(channel_sq_sums / n_pixels - means ** 2)
134	        stds = np.clip(stds, 1e-6, None)
135	        self._global_norm = (means.tolist(), stds.tolist())
136	
137	        print(f"Global norm stats — mean: {means}, std: {stds}")
138	
139	        # Create separate datasets: train with augmentation, val without
140	        train_full = EQDataset(self.data_dir, training_mode=True,
141	                               cache_in_ram=False, global_norm=self._global_norm)
142	        val_full = EQDataset(self.data_dir, training_mode=False,
143	                             cache_in_ram=False, global_norm=self._global_norm)
144	
145	        self.train_dataset = Subset(train_full, train_indices)
146	        self.val_dataset = Subset(val_full, val_indices)
147	
148	    def train_dataloader(self):
149	        return DataLoader(
150	            self.train_dataset,
151	            batch_size=self.batch_size,
152	            shuffle=True,
153	            num_workers=self.num_workers,
154	            pin_memory=True,
155	            persistent_workers=True if self.num_workers > 0 else False
156	        )
157	
158	    def val_dataloader(self):
159	        return DataLoader(
160	            self.val_dataset,
161	            batch_size=self.batch_size,
162	            shuffle=False,
163	            num_workers=self.num_workers,
164	            pin_memory=True,
165	            persistent_workers=True if self.num_workers > 0 else False
166	        )
167	
168	
169	if __name__ == "__main__":
170	    test_dir = Path("dataset")
171	
172	    print(f"Instantiating DataModule for directory: {test_dir}")
173	    dm = EQDataModule(data_dir=test_dir, batch_size=16, num_workers=0)
174	
175	    print("Running setup()...")
176	    dm.setup()
177	
178	    print(f"Train size: {len(dm.train_dataset)} samples")
179	    print(f"Val size: {len(dm.val_dataset)} samples")
180	
181	    x_batch, y_batch = next(iter(dm.train_dataloader()))
182	    print(f"\n--- Batch Shapes ---")
183	    print(f"x shape: {x_batch.shape}  y shape: {y_batch.shape}")
184	    print(f"x per-channel mean: {x_batch.mean(dim=(0,2,3)).tolist()}")
185	    print(f"x per-channel std:  {x_batch.std(dim=(0,2,3)).tolist()}")
186

import os
import random
import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader, random_split, Subset
import pytorch_lightning as pl
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path

# Import normalization from model so labels are always in [0,1]
from model_cnn import normalize_params


class EQDataset(Dataset):
    """
    Dataset for loading synthetic EQ `.npz` files.
    Supports two schema formats:
      Format A (pre-processed):  keys 'mel_stack' [3,128,431] + 'label' [24] (already normalized)
      Format B (raw generator):  keys 'dry_mel' [128,431] + 'wet_mel' [128,431] + 'labels' [24] (raw Hz/dB/Q)
    """
    def __init__(self, data_dir: str | Path, training_mode: bool = False,
                 cache_in_ram: bool = False, global_norm: tuple | None = None):
        super().__init__()
        self.data_dir = Path(data_dir)
        self.training_mode = training_mode
        self.cache_in_ram = cache_in_ram
        self.files = sorted(self.data_dir.rglob("*.npz"))
        if not self.files:
            raise FileNotFoundError(f"No .npz files found in {self.data_dir}")

        # Detect schema from first file
        sample = np.load(self.files[0])
        self._has_mel_stack = "mel_stack" in sample
        self._label_key = "label" if "label" in sample else "labels"

        # Global normalization stats: (mean[3], std[3]) per channel
        self._global_mean = None
        self._global_std = None
        if global_norm is not None:
            self._global_mean = torch.tensor(global_norm[0], dtype=torch.float32).view(3, 1, 1)
            self._global_std = torch.tensor(global_norm[1], dtype=torch.float32).view(3, 1, 1)

        if self.cache_in_ram:
            self._cache = [np.load(f) for f in self.files]

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self.files)

    def _build_mel_stack(self, data: np.lib.npyio.NpzFile) -> np.ndarray:
        """Build 3-channel mel_stack [3, 128, 431] from whatever keys are present."""
        if self._has_mel_stack:
            return data["mel_stack"]

        # Format B: reconstruct from dry_mel + wet_mel
        dry = data["dry_mel"]   # [128, 431]
        wet = data["wet_mel"]   # [128, 431]
        diff = wet - dry
        return np.stack([wet, dry, diff], axis=0).astype(np.float32)

    def __getitem__(self, idx: int) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        if self.cache_in_ram:
            data = self._cache[idx]
        else:
            data = np.load(self.files[idx])

        x = torch.tensor(self._build_mel_stack(data), dtype=torch.float32)
        y = torch.tensor(data[self._label_key], dtype=torch.float32)

        # Normalize raw labels to [0,1] so Sigmoid output can match.
        # Detect raw vs pre-normalized: raw labels have values > 1.0 (e.g., freq in Hz).
        # Pre-normalized datasets already have labels in [0,1] — skip to avoid
        # double-normalization which would compress the target range.
        if y.max() > 1.5:
            y = normalize_params(y)

        # Global normalization of mel-spectrogram input
        if self._global_mean is not None:
            x = (x - self._global_mean) / self._global_std.clamp(min=1e-6)

        # Training augmentation: frequency masking (SpecAugment-style)
        if self.training_mode:
            n_mel_bins = x.shape[1]
            for _ in range(random.randint(1, 3)):
                f_start = random.randint(0, n_mel_bins - 1)
                f_width = random.randint(1, min(8, n_mel_bins - f_start))
                x[:, f_start:f_start + f_width, :] = 0.0

        return x, y


class EQDataModule(pl.LightningDataModule):
    """
    Lightning DataModule mapping the EQDataset into Train and Val loaders
    with standard 80/20 splits.
    """
    def __init__(self, data_dir: str | Path, batch_size: int = 128, num_workers: int = 8):
        super().__init__()
        self.data_dir = data_dir
        self.batch_size = batch_size
        self.num_workers = num_workers

        # These will be populated in setup()
        self.train_dataset = None
        self.val_dataset = None
        self._global_norm = None

    def setup(self, stage: str = None):
        """Instantiates the dataset and creates the 80/20 split."""
        full_dataset = EQDataset(self.data_dir)

        train_size = int(0.8 * len(full_dataset))
        val_size = len(full_dataset) - train_size

        # Get reproducible indices for the split (same seed as before)
        generator = torch.Generator().manual_seed(42)
        indices = torch.randperm(len(full_dataset), generator=generator).tolist()
        train_indices = indices[:train_size]
        val_indices = indices[train_size:]

        # Compute global normalization statistics from training data ONLY.
        sample_indices = random.Random(42).sample(train_indices, min(500, len(train_indices)))
        channel_sums = np.zeros(3, dtype=np.float64)
        channel_sq_sums = np.zeros(3, dtype=np.float64)
        n_pixels = 0
        for idx in sample_indices:
            data = np.load(full_dataset.files[idx])
            mel = full_dataset._build_mel_stack(data).astype(np.float64)
            for c in range(3):
                channel_sums[c] += mel[c].sum()
                channel_sq_sums[c] += (mel[c] ** 2).sum()
            n_pixels += mel.shape[1] * mel.shape[2]

        means = channel_sums / n_pixels
        stds = np.sqrt(channel_sq_sums / n_pixels - means ** 2)
        stds = np.clip(stds, 1e-6, None)
        self._global_norm = (means.tolist(), stds.tolist())

        print(f"Global norm stats — mean: {means}, std: {stds}")

        # Create separate datasets: train with augmentation, val without
        train_full = EQDataset(self.data_dir, training_mode=True,
                               cache_in_ram=False, global_norm=self._global_norm)
        val_full = EQDataset(self.data_dir, training_mode=False,
                             cache_in_ram=False, global_norm=self._global_norm)

        self.train_dataset = Subset(train_full, train_indices)
        self.val_dataset = Subset(val_full, val_indices)

    def train_dataloader(self):
        return DataLoader(
            self.train_dataset,
            batch_size=self.batch_size,
            shuffle=True,
            num_workers=self.num_workers,
            pin_memory=True,
            persistent_workers=True if self.num_workers > 0 else False
        )

    def val_dataloader(self):
        return DataLoader(
            self.val_dataset,
            batch_size=self.batch_size,
            shuffle=False,
            num_workers=self.num_workers,
            pin_memory=True,
            persistent_workers=True if self.num_workers > 0 else False
        )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_dir = Path("dataset")

    print(f"Instantiating DataModule for directory: {test_dir}")
    dm = EQDataModule(data_dir=test_dir, batch_size=16, num_workers=0)

    print("Running setup()...")
    dm.setup()

    print(f"Train size: {len(dm.train_dataset)} samples")
    print(f"Val size: {len(dm.val_dataset)} samples")

    x_batch, y_batch = next(iter(dm.train_dataloader()))
    print(f"\n--- Batch Shapes ---")
    print(f"x shape: {x_batch.shape}  y shape: {y_batch.shape}")
    print(f"x per-channel mean: {x_batch.mean(dim=(0,2,3)).tolist()}")
    print(f"x per-channel std:  {x_batch.std(dim=(0,2,3)).tolist()}")

import torch
import pytorch_lightning as pl
from pytorch_lightning.callbacks import ModelCheckpoint, LearningRateMonitor, EarlyStopping
from pytorch_lightning.loggers import CSVLogger
from pathlib import Path

from data_module import EQDataModule
from model_cnn import EQRegressor

MAX_EPOCHS = 100


def main():
    torch.set_float32_matmul_precision('high')

    dm = EQDataModule(data_dir=Path("dataset"), batch_size=128, num_workers=8)
    model = EQRegressor(lr=5e-4, warmup_epochs=5, max_epochs=MAX_EPOCHS)

    checkpoint_callback = ModelCheckpoint(
        dirpath="checkpoints",
        filename="eq-cnn-{epoch:02d}-{val_loss:.4f}",
        save_top_k=5,
        monitor="val_loss",
        mode="min",
    )
    early_stop = EarlyStopping(monitor="val_loss", patience=15, mode="min", verbose=True)
    lr_monitor = LearningRateMonitor(logging_interval='epoch')

    trainer = pl.Trainer(
        max_epochs=MAX_EPOCHS,
        accelerator="auto",
        devices=1,
        precision="16-mixed",
        gradient_clip_val=1.0,
        callbacks=[checkpoint_callback, early_stop, lr_monitor],
        logger=CSVLogger("logs", name="eq_cnn"),
        log_every_n_steps=10,
        enable_progress_bar=False,
    )

    # NOTE: If model architecture has changed, delete checkpoints/ before running.
    ckpt_dir = Path("checkpoints")
    ckpts = sorted(ckpt_dir.glob("*.ckpt"), key=lambda f: float(f.stem.split("val_loss=")[-1]))
    resume = str(ckpts[0]) if ckpts else None
    if resume:
        print(f"=== Resuming from: {resume} ===")

    trainer.fit(model, datamodule=dm, ckpt_path=resume)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
